coredata.dc:title: A convenient fluorescence sensor of tetracycline based on B, N codoped carbon dots/polymer composite film
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yingying
Guan, Rentian
Zhang, Shuai
Fan, Xiaoyu
Liu, Wenjing
Zhang, Keying
Shao, Xiaodong
Li, Xia
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: A convenient fluorescence sensor of tetracycline (TC) was constructed based on carbon dots (CDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel film. The immobilization of CDs in PVA carrier can stabilize the fluorescence of CDs by inhibiting the fluorescence quench due to the aggregation of CDs with time. CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. CDs showed bright blue fluorescence with the quantum yield of 0.35. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by TC owing to the inner filter effect. The linear range for TC detection was 0–350 µM and the limit of detection was 0.17 µM. To test conveniently, PVA film was employed to upload CDs. Therefore, a novel sensor for TC was constructed in a visual mode. By comparison with the most of previous works, the present method displayed higher sensitivity and better selectivity. The results suggest that the present sensor has potential applications in the real-time detection of TC in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline
Carbon dots
Poly (vinyl alcohol) film
Inner filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs (A) and emission spectra of CDs and CDs-PVA film (inset: photographs of CDs and CDs-PVA film under daylight and UV lamp) (B).
TEM image of CDs (A) (inset: size distribution), SEM images of PVA film (B) and CDs-PVA film (C).

Fluorescence (A) (the concentration of TC is 250 µM) and absorption (B) spectra of CDs, TC and CDs + TC, fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of TC (C) and corresponding linear relationship between the fluorescence decrease and TC concentration (D), and photographs of CDs with varying concentrations of TC under the daylight and UV lamp (E).

Photographs of CDs-PVA film (A), with different CDs volumes (from left to right, 1000, 800, 400, 100 μL/mL) (B), with different TC concentrations (from left to right, 0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 mg/mL) (C), and with milk samples spiked by TC with different concentrations (from left to right, blank, only milk samples, spiked by TC with 0.01, 0.1, and 1.0 mg/mL) (D) under UV and daylight lamp.
Schematic illustration of CDs (A) and CDs/PVA film (B) synthesis and application in tetracycline detection.
Results of TC detection in real samples of milk and milk powder.

head.ce:title: A convenient fluorescence sensor of tetracycline based on B, N codoped carbon dots/polymer composite film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian 710077, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian 710077, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A convenient fluorescence sensor of tetracycline (TC) was constructed based on carbon dots (CDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel film. The immobilization of CDs in PVA carrier can stabilize the fluorescence of CDs by inhibiting the fluorescence quench due to the aggregation of CDs with time. CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. CDs showed bright blue fluorescence with the quantum yield of 0.35. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by TC owing to the inner filter effect. The linear range for TC detection was 0–350 µM and the limit of detection was 0.17 µM. To test conveniently, PVA film was employed to upload CDs. Therefore, a novel sensor for TC was constructed in a visual mode. By comparison with the most of previous works, the present method displayed higher sensitivity and better selectivity. The results suggest that the present sensor has potential applications in the real-time detection of TC in food analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Since first discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), carbon dots (CDs) as competitive candidates among carbon-based nanomaterials have received considerable attentions. As zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with almost spherical morphology, they own universal size less than 20 nm with abundant functional groups on their surface (Zhu et al., 2015; 2017). The multiple groups can help CDs to be modified easily with many organic or polymeric molecules (Yang, Jiang, Qiu, Jiang, & Zuo, 2015), and to construct multifarious sensors. The carbon atoms in the core of CDs consist of sp2/sp3 electronic structures and display amorphous carbon form or graphite lattice (Bhattacharya, Das, & Mukherjee, 2016; Zhang, 2016). By comparison with some traditional cadmium-based quantum dots; CDs exhibit higher photoluminescence (PL) stability, better biocompatibility, as well as lower toxicity and cost (Xia, Zhu, Feng, Yang, & Yang, 2019; Yuan et al., 2016). These properties afford CDs economical promise in practical applications.

Despite the rapid advancements, there are challenges in the development of CDs, one of which is the synthesis of CDs with high PL quantum yield (QY). The extra surface passivation and composition of CDs are often required for optical property tuning, such as organic molecules, heteroatoms doping, as well as polymers (Peng, Ji, Zhou, Zhao, & Leblanc, 2020). Polymers are ideal materials for compounding with CDs because polymer can provide mechanical support and CDs can disperse well in polymer's structure. In this case, the dispersion of CDs can prevent their PL quenching (Du, Wang, Wu, & Chen, 2021). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) has repeating CH2CH(OH) units. PVA is generally considered a nontoxic material. Due to its low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and water absorption, PVA is widely used in tissue engineering and medical applications (Hu, Gu, Hu, Deng, & Wang, 2016; Pathan et al., 2015). PVA has also been used as an immobilization carrier for reinforced composites to improve its mechanical performance by complex with other materials such as graphene (Zhao, Zhang, Chen, & Lu, 2010) and graphene oxide (Kashyap, Pratihar, & Behera, 2016; Qi et al., 2013). Since CDs have plenty of functional groups including graphene oxide on their surface, they can also interact strongly with PVA chain and improve the mechanical properties of PVA hydrogel (Kashyap et al., 2016). More importantly, incorporating CDs into PVA hydrogel can expand the application range in fluorescence sensors for PVA. Furthermore, the immobilization of CDs in PVA carrier can stabilize the fluorescence of CDs inhibiting the fluorescence quench deriving from the aggregation of CDs with time.

Tetracyclines (TCs) are a group of antibiotics derived from Streptomyces spp. with a broad-spectrum activity. In veterinary medicine the most commonly used TCs are tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC) and doxycycline (DC). TC is a broad-spectrum antimicrobial drug for treatment of bacterial infections in humans or animals (Feng, Zhong, Miao, & Yang, 2015; Wang, Cheng, & Wang, 2018). TC is also used for feed additive in the agriculture sector (Jia et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019). The excessive use of TC caused global concern due to its many side effects. The long-time intake of TC in food stuffs may cause some serious risks; such as drug resistance of microbial strains, allergic or toxic reactions (Gong, Li, Qing, Zhang, & Feng, 2019). Thus, it is necessary to explore simple and effective means for the detection of TC in food products. Different analytical approaches for the detection of TC have been developed, including chromatography (Al-Afy, Sereshti, Hijazi, & Rashidi, 2018), electrophoresis (Zhou, Deng, Shi, & Zhou, 2017), electrochemistry (Kim, Kim, Niazi, & Gu, 2010; Tang et al., 2018), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Gong et al., 2019). These techniques are time consuming and need expensive apparatus and sophisticate skills. The optical approaches, by contrast, are superior due to their excellent sensitivity and selectivity, easy operation, and fast response (Li et al., 2019; Raizada et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). The fluorescence sensing methods based on CDs are widely used for the determination of TCs such as N, S co-doped CDs (Zhao et al., 2018; 2020), nitrogen doped CDs (Yan et al., 2019), and carbon nitride quantum dots (Hu et al., 2020). All of these assays are carried out in aqueous media, and they are not strict sensors.

In this work, a novel sensor of TC was designed using CDs/PVA composite film based on B, N co-doped CDs. PVA was selected as a supporting matrix due to its good comprehensive properties including strong mechanical strength and thermal stability and excellent carrier property (Lim et al., 2015). Since there are many strong hydrogen bonds among PVA chains, the fabrication of PVA-based matrix was mostly conducted in solutions. Herein, urea was added to the PVA system to obtain strong mechanical strength. CDs were prepared from 3-aminophenylboronic acid and citric acid using a hydrothermal method. CDs were treated with PVA to form a polymer composite film with bright blue fluorescence. Since the fluorescence of CDs/PVA can be quenched easily due to the inner filter effect (IFE), a convenient, sensitive, selective, as well as portable sensor was constructed to determine TC in real samples. The preparation of CDs and assay process of TC were illustrated in a schematic diagram (Scheme 1 ).

CDs were prepared by a Teflon-sealed autoclave reactor in a vacuum drying oven. An LS-55 spectrofluorometer was used to obtain fluorescence spectra and intensity. A Lambda 750 spectrophotometer was employed to measure absorption spectra. A JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was selected to study the morphology and size of CDs. TEM worked with 200 kV acceleration voltage, and a copper grid coated with carbon was used for sample suspension. A thermo Fisher Scientific FIB-SEM GX4 was selected to study the morphology of CDs and CDs/PVA film. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs was studied by a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer. A K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrometer was employed to confirm the chemical bonding state of elements of CDs. A Coolpix 4500 digital camera was used to capture digital photos.

3-aminophenylboronic acid, citric acid and PVA were ordered from Shanghai Perfemiker Material Tech Co., Ltd. Common salts, acids and bases were obtained in Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. There were CaCl2, Fe(NO3)3, MgCl2, NaCl, KCl, ZnCl2, HCl, HOAc, NaOH, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NaOAc, Tris, HEPES, and quinine sulfate (QS). Amino acids, proteins and other biological species were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Reagent Co., Ltd. They included bovine serum albumin (BSA), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), glutataione (GSH), lysine (Lys), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr) and aspartic (Asp). Some antibiotics with similar structures as TC were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Tech Co., Ltd. They included sulfadiazine (SD), doxycycline hyclate (DOX), oxytetracycline (OTC), streptomycin (STR), penicillin G, Na (Pen G Na), erythromycin (ERY), clindamycin hydrochloride (CLI), chloroamphenicol (CHL), cefotaxime sodium salt (CTX), chlorotetracycline hydrochloride (CTE), azithromycin (AZM). All chemicals are analytical grade, and they can be used directly without further purification, if there are no special instructions.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal strategy. Briefly, 5 mL deionized water was added to the mixture of 4.8 mg 3-aminophenylboronic acid and 4.8 mg citric acid with ultrasound for 2 min. After transferring to a 25 mL reactor, the mixture was heated in a vacuum oven at 180 °C for 6 h. The product was purified in a centrifuge for 5 min to remove reaction residue and agglomerated particles. The solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h by 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove excess reactants. Afterwards, the obtained pale-yellow transparent solution was dried in a vacuum drying oven to obtain the final CDs powder. The powder product of CDs (1.0 mg·mL−1) was redispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

A drop-coating method was used to prepare the CDs/PVA composite film. Briefly, PVA (100 mg·mL−1) was mixed in CDs solution. After the mixture was dissolved completely, urea (65 mg·mL−1) was added to the solution as a crosslinking agent for PVA. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was dropped into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) plate for water evaporation.

TC was detected in aqueous media with pH at 6.64. Firstly, standard solutions of TC were prepared with different concentrations. Then, 250 μL CDs (0.02 mg⋅mL−1) was mixed with 5 μL TC standard solution in a blender by shaking for 5 min. The emission spectra of CDs were recorded with optimal excitation wavelength in the presence and absence of TC.

The QY of CDs was calculated according to an established procedure. QS in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY of 54%) was chosen as a reference. The emission (λex 378 nm) and absorption spectra were recorded by fluorescence and UV–vis spectrophotometers, respectively. All of the integrated areas for the emission peaks were obtained by the origin 8.0 software. The QY of CDs (λex 378 nm) was calculated by the following equation:
QYX = QYST (mX / mST) (η2 X / η2 ST) (1)

In eq. 1, m is the slope (the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance), η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “ST” refers to a standard reagent with known QY and “X” is for the sample.

CDs products were used to detect TC in food samples in aqueous media. The food samples were purchased from local supermarket (Liaocheng, China), which were pretreated according to the published work with minor modification (Qu, Sun, Liu, Zhao, & You, 2016). Briefly, 2.0 mL milk and milk powder (100 mg·mL−1) were mixed in 4.0 mL ultrapure water under vigorous stirring. Then the mixture was heated in 90 °C for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature and centrifuge for 10 min (15,000 rpm), the supernatant was collected. The centrifuge was carried out three times using the same parameters. The solutions were filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane to move protein, and stored at 4 °C before use. TC was analyzed by the proposed sensing technique, and the recovery efficiency was calculated. To confirm the accurateness of the present method, HPLC-UV method was employed to analyze the same samples (GB/T 22990–2008, National food safety standards).

Fluorescence quenching means any process that decreases the quantum yield or the fluorescence intensity from a fluorophore. Various molecular interactions can lead to fluorescence quenching. These include energy transfer, molecular rearrangements, collisional quenching and so on. The intrinsical issue of fluorimetric procedures is the absorption of exciting and/or emission by dissolved materials, including the fluorophore itself. This procedure is called the inner filter effect (IFE) (Puchalski, Morra, & von Wandruszka, 1991). Currently, IFE has become an important non-irradiation energy conversion model of fluorometry for many applications in chemical sensing and biosensing (Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018). The establishment of sensing system based on IFE requires absorber and fluorescer as two optical units. There are several points to be considered. Among them, one point used widely is that the absorption spectra of absorber should have enough overlap with the excitation and/or emission spectra of fluorescer. Herein, CDs and TC are the fluorescer and absorber for the IFE system, respectively. There is an obvious overlap for the excitation spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of TC, as shown in Fig. S1A (in Supplementary Material). In addition, the relationship of F0/F and TC concentration was illustrated under different temperatures (Fig. S1B, in Supplementary Material). It is shown that the slope of the plots increases with temperature, which means the diffusion or collision effect may be the main factor for the fluorescence quenching of CDs as illustrated in the literature (Lakowicz, 1999). Furthermore, the fluorescence decay was also measured (Fig. S1C, in Supplementary Material). The lifetime of CDs varies from 3.85 μs to 3.72 μs. It proves the collision quenching is the main effect for TC on the fluorescence of CDs. And then the collision and absorption effects of TC lead to the quenching of CDs fluorescence. Thus, an IFE-based collision quenching system was constructed for the detection of TC by employing CDs as fluorescer.
To acquire the optimal performance of CDs, the experimental conditions including time, temperature, and molar ratio of precursors for CDs synthesis were investigated, respectively.

Firstly, the reaction time and temperature for CDs synthesis were tested. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was obtained with different reaction time (4–8 h) (Fig. S2A, in Supplementary Material). It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs reaches the maximum at 6 h. The effect of reaction temperature on the fluorescence of CDs was studied in the range of 160–200 °C (Fig. S2B, in Supplementary Material). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs goes up as the temperature varies from 160 °C to 180 °C and goes down in the range of 180–200 °C. Fluorescence intensity arrives at a peak with reaction under 180 °C. Therefore, the reaction time of 6 h and the temperature of 180 °C were used in subsequent experiments as optimum conditions.

Secondly, the molar ratio of citric acid and 3-aminophenylboronic acid was investigated (Fig. S2C, in Supplementary Material). It can be observed that the relative fluorescence intensity gets to the maximum when the molar ratio is 1:1 for citric acid and 3-aminophenylboronic acid. Thus, citric acid and 3-aminophenylboronic acid were used as precursor materials with equal mole for CDs synthesis.

The schematic illustration is shown in Scheme 1 for the synthetic route of CDs and CDs-PVA composite film. CDs products were obtained from 3-aminophenylboronic acid and citric acid by a hydrothermal method. The synthesis was carried out under optimal reaction temperature and time at 180 °C and 6 h, respectively. CDs-PVA polymer composite film was obtained by drop-coating method. As shown in Fig. 1 , the optical properties of CDs are explored. From Fig. 1A, we can see the UV–vis absorption spectrum shows obvious absorption bands at 231 nm and 280 nm, respectively. The first band of 231 nm is the π-π* transition of the sp2 in aromatic structures, while the peak at 280 nm was identified as n-π* absorption band of CO (Liu et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2020). The emission spectra of CDs solution and CDs-PVA film are shown in Fig. 1B. It can be observed that the spectrum of CDs-PVA film has a little red shift but still in the blue emission zone. It may be due to the interaction between the functional groups on the surface of CDs and the groups of PVA, resulting in a little red shift. The digital photos of CDs and CDs-PVA film are inserted in Fig. 1B. CDs solution appears colorless and the CDs-PVA polymer composite film displays pale-yellow under natural light. Under UV lamp (365 nm), however, CDs and CDs-PVA polymer composite film show bright blue.

To obtain an optimum excite light, a series of fluorescence spectra were recorded by varying the excitation wavelength for CDs and CDs-PVA (Fig. S3, in Supplementary Material). For CDs solution, when the excitation wavelength moves from 348 to 378 nm, the peak fluorescence intensity increases significantly, while it goes down with the excitation wavelength ranging from 378 to 408 nm (Fig. S3, in Supplementary Material). Therefore, 378 nm is the optimum excitation wavelength for the highest emission peak intensity, which is used in the subsequent experiments. For CDs-PVA film, when the excitation wavelength increases from 348 nm to 408 nm, the emission peak signal fluctuates up and down. It can also be seen that there is an obvious red shift in the wavelength range of 398–408 nm. The result agrees well with that of previous report. It may be ascribed to the distinctive nanoparticles with different sizes, different emissive traps and different polyaromatic fluorophores on the structure of CDs core (Fu, Chen, Liu, & Ma, 2011). As expected, the PVA film itself has no fluorescence (Fig. S2C, in Supplementary Material). Therefore, it can be deduced that CDs and PVA are successfully composited. As illustrated in Fig. S2D (in Supplementary Material), the QY of CDs was calculated as 0.35 with QS as a reference.

TEM images indicate that CDs are spherical and well dispersed without obvious aggregation in aqueous media (Fig. 2 A). The diameter of CDs is in the range of 1.2–2.4 nm, and the most particle size distribution is 1.8 ± 0.2 nm (inset of Fig. 2A). SEM images of PVA and CDs-PVA films are shown in Fig. 2B and C. It can be found that there are nanofibers for both PVA and CDs-PVA. By comparison, the surface of CDs-PVA film is rougher and more porous than that of PVA film. The porous structure may be helpful for the adsorption of other species on the CDs-PVA film.

Fig. S4A (in Supplementary Material) exhibits the XRD pattern of CDs products. XRD pattern displays a broad peak around 24.2°, which is an indication that CDs form amorphous rather than crystal structure.

The functional groups on the surface of CDs were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. S4B (in Supplementary Material) exhibits the FT-IR spectrum of CDs product. The broad absorption peak of 3000–3600 cm−1 is NH and OH stretching vibrations. It demonstrates the richness of –NH2 and –OH on CDs surface, which is greatly beneficial to the hydrophilic properties of CDs. The weak band at 1723 cm−1 and a strong one at 1645 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of CO group. The bands at 1593 and 1495 cm−1 indicate the existence of CC and CN stretching vibrations. The bands at 1412, 1275, 1208 and 1128 cm−1 may be ascribed to the typical B-O/CN, CNC/COC stretching vibrations, B-OH bending vibrations, and B-C/C-OH stretching vibrations, respectively (Bian et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2018). The peaks at 950–650 cm−1 belong to bending vibrations of CH in benzene ring. It can be seen from the results that there exist many groups on CDs surface, and N, B elements are successfully doped into CDs by a one-pot hydrothermal method.

XPS was further performed to reveal the abundance of B and N doping and the binding mode of C, O, N, and B in the nanostructure of CDs. Fig. S5A (in Supplementary Material) displays clearly the full spectrum of binding energy of CDs. Four peaks can be observed, which are at 191.1, 284.8, 400.0, and 532.3 eV, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s is shown in Fig. S5B (in Supplementary Material). It can be divided into four peaks at 531.3, 531.9, 532.6, 533.5 eV, which attributed to CO, COC/OCN, C-OH/OC-O, HO-CO, respectively. In Fig. S5C (in Supplementary Material), the peaks of N 1 s spectra at 398.9, 399.6, 400.2, and 401.2 eV, which belong to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and CN, respectively. The peaks of C 1 s high-resolution spectra at 284.4 eV, 284.9 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.8 eV (Fig. S5D, in Supplementary Material). These peaks are ascribed to CC/CH, CC, CO/CN, O-CO, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of B 1 s can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 190.7, 191.1, and 191.6 eV (Fig. S5E, in Supplementary Material), which are attributed to B-C, B-N, and B-O, respectively (Hu et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Moreover, the composition fraction of C, O, N, and B elements are revealed in Fig. S5A (in Supplementary Material), which are 70.27%, 21.41%, 4.96%, and 3.35%, respectively.

To obtain optimum performance of the present system for TC detection, some sensing conditions were investigated including buffer species, pH and ion strength. In the procedure, the fluorescence intensity was also employed as an important parameter to evaluate the conditions.

Firstly, pH was tested. The fluorescence intensity for CDs and CDs + TC was measured under different pH values (Fig. S6A, in Supplementary Material). For CDs, no significant fluorescence change is observed at pH 3.0–10.0. A similar result is acquired for CDs + TC system where the fluorescence intensity changes weakly in the same pH range. It demonstrates that the fluorescence activity is stable for both CDs and CDs + TC systems in aqueous medium with a wide pH range.

Secondly, the buffer species were studied. Different species were employed to adjust the solution pH including HEPES, PB, PBS, Tris-HCl, and HOAc-NaOAc, respectively. Fig. S6B (in Supplementary Material) depicts the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs + TC in different buffer solutions. It can be found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs in ultrapure water is higher than that in buffer solutions. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CDs + TC is the lowest in ultrapure water. Considering the simplicity and easy control of the proposal, distilled water with pH 6.64 was selected as optimum medium in the subsequent experiments.

Finally, the influence of ion strength was carried out in NaCl solution with different concentrations (Fig. S6C, in Supplementary Material). It can be seen that there is no significant change for the fluorescence signal of CDs in NaCl solution. Therefore, the ion strength shows no effect on the fluorescence measurement.

The fluorescence and absorption spectrum of CDs were investigated with and without TC (Fig. 3 A and B). Obviously, CDs exhibit obviously strong fluorescence, while the fluorescence of TC is very weak. However, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched significantly by TC. By comparison with the absorption spectra of CDs and TC (Fig. 3B), there is almost no obvious new peak and wavelength shift in the absorption spectrum of CDs + TC. There may be no chemical and bind reactions between CDs and TC. Therefore, an inference is that there is probably no complexes formation between CDs and TC.

The sensitivity for TC detection was studied under optimum synthesis and sensing conditions. The conditions include reaction under 180 °C for 6 h, molar ratio of precursors at 1:1, distilled water with pH at 6.64, excitation/emission wavelength at 378/425 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases in the presence of TC (0, 1.8, 2.5, 3.6, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200, 250, 300, and 350 µM) (Fig. 3C). Fig. 3C illustrates clearly that the fluorescence of CDs is quenched by TC. There is a good linear relationship between the corresponding fluorescence change (F0-F) and the concentration of TC (R2 = 0.9962) ranging from 0 µM to 350 µM (Fig. 3D). As a result, the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated as 0.17 µM by 3.29S B/m equation according to the recommendation of IUPAC. In the criterion, S B and m are the standard deviation of the blank (n = 5) and the slope of the calibration curve, respectively. In addition, in order to see the quenching effect of TC on the fluorescence of CDs more intuitively, the images of CDs with and without TC are shown in Fig. 3E with the illumination of daylight and UV lamp (365 nm). It can be seen that as the TC concentration increases, the CDs fluorescence is quenched gradually. It indicates that CDs products as efficient probes can be applied in fluorometry methods. The results for TC detection are summarized in Table S1 (in Supplementary Material) for which various fluorometry methods are used with different carbon-based dots. By comparison, B, N-codoped CDs in the present system shows lower LOD for TC detection.

The CDs-PVA film was also used to detect TC. Firstly, the homogeneity test was carried out for CDs-PVA film. From Fig. 4 A, the photos of CDs-PVA film in UV light are almost the same for four different zones. Secondly, the upload of CDs was investigated. The blue fluorescence is brighter with higher upload of CDs on the CDs-PVA film (Fig. 4B). And then 1 mL CDs was selected to mix with 1 mL PVA. Thirdly, the CDs-PVA film was soaked in TC solutions with different concentrations. It can be obviously observed that the fluorescence becomes weaker when the concentration of TC increases. The blue fluorescence of CDs-PVA film disappears for 1 mg/mL TC solution (Fig. 4C). It can be deduced that CDs-PVA film can be used to detect TC in a visual mode.

The specificity is a critical feature for a sensing method to evaluate its capability. Some common metal ions, amino acids and other substances were tested (Fig. S7A, in Supplementary Material). It can be observed that the fluorescence of CDs can be significantly quenched by TC, while other species only cause slight fluorescence change, even with concentration 2 times higher than that of TC.

Influence of some possible interfering antibiotics was also studied. They include SD, DOX, OTC, STR, Pen G Na, ERY, CLI, CHL, CTX, CTE, and AZM. Among TCs including TC, OTC, DOX, and CTE (250 µM), only TC can obviously quench the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S7B, in Supplementary Material). Other antibiotics do not affect CDs fluorescence even with higher concentration (500 µM). It illustrates that the detection system displays the specificity for TC rather than for other TCs with similar structures.

TCs are a group of antibiotics derived from Streptomyces spp. with a broad spectrum antibacterial activity. TC is widely used in food, medicine, biological and other fields. Therefore, TC is an important substance in our life. TC was tested in real samples, such as milk and milk powder employing the present method. The milk and milk powder samples were pretreated according to the procedure listed in experimental section and then diluted 5 times for subsequent analysis. Recovery experiments were carried out using spiked samples of TC with different concentrations in milk and milk powder (Table 1 ). It can be observed that the recovery values of TC in milk samples range from 94.3% to 105.6% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 7.06%. The same samples were also detected by a national standard method. By comparison, the results obtained from the present and reference methods are similar and the t-test results also show good consistence of the two methods. Moreover, the CDs-PVA film was employed to detect TC in milk samples. As illustrated in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence of CDs-PVA film is almost same for blank and milk sample without TC. The result agrees well with that of the test in aqueous media. When the CDs-PVA film is soaked in the milk samples spiked with TC, the fluorescence appears weaker and weaker with the TC concentration increase. Therefore, this method has some obvious advantages including portable and convenient operation for the determination of TC in milk, and has a broad application prospect.

In this work, a sensitive, specific, and convenient fluorescence sensor was constructed for TC assay. It was achieved by incorporating CDs into PVA film. The immobilization of CDs in PVA carrier can stabilize the fluorescence of CDs inhibiting the fluorescence quench deriving from the aggregation of CDs with time. B, N-codoped CDs was synthesized by a solvothermal method using citric acid and 3-aminophenylboronic acid as precursors. CDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence. Due to the internal filtering effect of TC, it can quench the fluorescence of CDs. The quenching fluorescence intensity of CDs shows a good linear response to the concentration of TC ranging from 0 µM to 350 µM. The fluorescence system can be used in both aqueous media and PVA film for TC detection. It is successfully used to analyze complex samples of milk to examine the concentration of TC. The result is of good accuracy and it indicates that the approach may have good applicability and great potential in real time sensing of TC in real samples.

Yingying Hu: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Rentian Guan: Formal analysis. Shuai Zhang: Formal analysis. Xiaoyu Fan: Formal analysis. Wenjing Liu: Formal analysis. Keying Zhang: Formal analysis. Xiaodong Shao: Visualization. Xia Li: Formal analysis. Qiaoli Yue: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by Natural Science Foundation of China (91543206) and research foundation of Liaocheng University (318050022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131287.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
